content:
# Leviticus Chapter 9 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 62v–63r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_009_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_009_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 93–94 | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_009_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_009_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 9 narrates the climactic eighth day of the ordination period — the day on which the newly consecrated priesthood performs its inaugural public service. The chapter divides structurally: (1) vv.1-6: Moshe's instructions to Aharon and the elders; (2) vv.7-14: Aharon's personal offerings — chattat and olah for himself; (3) vv.15-21: Aharon's offerings for the people — chattat, olah, minchah, shelamim; (4) v.22: Aaron's first priestly blessing; (5) v.23: Moshe and Aharon enter the tent of meeting, come out together, and bless the people; (6) v.24: fire comes out from before the LORD and consumes the offerings — the people shout and fall on their faces. The "eighth day" (ba-yom ha-shemini) frames the entire chapter as the theological culmination of the seven-day ordination: number eight in Hebrew thought signals a new beginning beyond the complete cycle of seven. MT and English versification are identical throughout.]*

---

**1** And it came to pass [vayehi] on the eighth day [ba-yom ha-shemini] [ba-yom ha-shemini — "on the eighth day"; the day following the completion of the seven-day ordination period (Lev 8:33-36); "eighth" in Hebrew numerical symbolism marks the inauguration of a new order beyond the complete cycle of seven] that Moshe [Moshe] called to Aharon [qara Moshe el-Aharon] and to his sons [ve-el-banav] and to the elders of Yisra'el [ve-el ziqney Yisra'el].

**2** And he said to Aharon [vayomer el-Aharon]: "Take for yourself [qach lekha] a calf [egel] of the herd [ben-baqar] for a sin offering [le-chattat] [chattat — "sin offering"; also functions as a purification offering, removing impurity and atoning for sin; the chattat for Aharon on this day corresponds to the sin-offering bull of his ordination (Lev 8:14)] and a ram [ve-ayil] for a burnt offering [le-olah] [olah — "burnt offering"; wholly consumed on the altar, given entirely to the LORD; from the root alah, "to go up"] without blemish [temimim] and bring them near [vehaqrev] before the LORD [lifney YHWH].

**3** "And to the sons of Yisra'el [ve-el-bney Yisra'el] you shall speak [tedabber], saying: 'Take [qechu] a male goat [se'ir-izzim] for a sin offering [le-chattat] and a calf [ve-egel] and a lamb [ve-keves] — sons of a year [bney shanah] — without blemish [temimim] — for a burnt offering [le-olah],

**4** and an ox [ve-shor] and a ram [ve-ayil] for peace offerings [li-shelamim] [shelamim — "peace offerings" or "well-being offerings"; from shalom, "completeness/peace"; shared meals in which portions go to the LORD (fat, blood), the priest, and the worshiper] to sacrifice before the LORD [lizebach lifney YHWH] and a grain offering [u-minchah] [minchah — "grain offering"; fine flour with oil and frankincense; presented alongside the animal offerings] mixed with oil [belulah va-shemen] — for today [ki ha-yom] the LORD is appearing to you [nir'ah YHWH aleikhem].'"

**5** And they brought [vayiqechu] what Moshe commanded [et asher tsivvah Moshe] to the front of the tent of meeting [el-pney ohel mo'ed] and the whole congregation drew near [vayyiqrevu kol-ha-edah] and they stood before the LORD [vaya'amdu lifney YHWH].

**6** And Moshe said [vayomer Moshe]: "This is the thing [zeh ha-davar] that the LORD commanded [asher tsivvah YHWH] — do it [ta'asu oto] and the glory of the LORD [u-khevod YHWH] [kevod YHWH — "the glory of the LORD"; the kabod — the weighty, luminous, manifest presence of the LORD (compare Ex 24:16-17; 40:34-35); the promise of Lev 9:4 ("the LORD is appearing to you") is here given its theological grounding: the appointed ritual obedience is the precondition for the divine appearance] will appear to you [yera'eh aleikhem]."

---

**7** And Moshe said to Aharon [vayomer Moshe el-Aharon]: "Draw near to the altar [qerav el-ha-mizbeach] and make your sin offering [va'aseh et-chattatekha] and your burnt offering [ve-et-olatekha] and make atonement for yourself [vekhapper ba'adkha] and on behalf of the people [u-va'ad ha-am] and make the offering of the people [ve'aseh et-qorban ha-am] and make atonement on their behalf [vekhapper ba'adam] — as the LORD commanded [ka'asher tsivvah YHWH]."

**8** And Aharon drew near to the altar [vayiqrav Aharon el-ha-mizbeach] and slaughtered the calf of the sin offering [vayishchat et-egel ha-chattat] which was for himself [asher lo].

**9** And the sons of Aharon [u-vney Aharon] presented the blood to him [hiqrivu et-ha-dam elav] and he dipped his finger in the blood [vayitbol etsba'o ba-dam] and put it [vayittten] upon the horns of the altar [al-qarnot ha-mizbeach] and the blood [ve-et-ha-dam] he poured out [yatsaq] at the base of the altar [el-yesod ha-mizbeach].

**10** But the fat [ve-et-ha-chelev] and the kidneys [ve-et-ha-kelayot] and the lobe from the liver [ve-et-ha-yoteret min-ha-kaved] from the sin offering [min-ha-chattat] he burned on the altar [hiqtir ha-mizbeachah] — as the LORD commanded Moshe [ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et-Moshe].

**11** And the flesh [ve-et-ha-basar] and the skin [ve-et-ha-or] he burned with fire [saraf ba-esh] outside the camp [michutz la-machaneh].

**12** And he slaughtered the burnt offering [vayishchat et-ha-olah] and the sons of Aharon [vayamtsi'u bney Aharon] handed him the blood [elav et-ha-dam] and he dashed it [vayizreqehu] against the altar [al-ha-mizbeach] on all sides [saviv].

**13** And the burnt offering [ve-et-ha-olah] they handed to him piece by piece [himtsi'u elav linetochiav] and the head [ve-et-ha-rosh] and he burned them on the altar [vayaqter ha-mizbeachah].

**14** And he washed the entrails [vayrachtsets et-ha-qerev] and the legs [ve-et-ha-kera'ayim] and burned them [vayaqter] on top of the burnt offering [al-ha-olah] on the altar [ha-mizbeachah].

---

**15** And he brought near the offering of the people [vayaqrev et-qorban ha-am] and he took the male goat of the sin offering [vayiqach et-se'ir ha-chattat] which was for the people [asher la-am] and slaughtered it [vayishchatehu] and purified it [vaychatte'ehu] [vaychatte'ehu — hiphil/piel of chata'; "he performed the chattat-rite upon it" / "he de-sinned it"; the same technical verb used in Lev 8:15 for the purification of the altar; here it indicates the sin-offering blood manipulation that cleanses the altar from any impurity carried by the people] like the first one [karishon].

**16** And he brought near the burnt offering [vayaqrev et-ha-olah] and made it according to the ordinance [vaya'aseha ka-mishpat] [ka-mishpat — "according to the ordinance/judgment"; mishpat here means the established rule of procedure for the burnt offering as specified in Lev 1:3-9].

**17** And he brought near the grain offering [vayaqrev et-ha-minchah] and filled his hand from it [vayemalle' kappo mimmenah] and burned it on the altar [vayaqter ha-mizbeachah] — besides [mil-levad] the burnt offering of the morning [olat ha-boqer] [olat ha-boqer — "the burnt offering of the morning"; the daily tamid offering prescribed in Ex 29:38-42; the people's inaugural offerings are thus presented in addition to, not instead of, the continual daily offering, establishing the principle that the regular worship is never displaced by special occasions].

**18** And he slaughtered the ox [vayishchat et-ha-shor] and the ram [ve-et-ha-ayil] — the sacrifice of the peace offerings [zevach ha-shelamim] which was for the people [asher la-am] and the sons of Aharon [vayamtsi'u bney Aharon] handed the blood to him [elav et-ha-dam] and he dashed it [vayizreqehu] against the altar [al-ha-mizbeach] on all sides [saviv].

**19** And the fat portions [ve-et-ha-chalavim] from the ox [min-ha-shor] and from the ram [u-min-ha-ayil] — the fat tail [ha-alyah] and the covering fat [ha-mekhasseh] and the kidneys [ve-ha-kelayot] and the lobe of the liver [ve-yoteret ha-kaved] [ha-alyah — "the fat tail"; the broad, fat-laden tail of the Levantine sheep (Ovis aries laticaudata); the fat tail is characteristic of the sheep species kept in ancient Israel and is reserved for the LORD along with the other fats, since חֵלֶב (chelev, "suet/fat") is prohibited for human consumption (Lev 3:17; 7:23)].

**20** And they placed the fat portions [vayasimu et-ha-chalavim] upon the breasts [al-he-chazot] and he burned the fat portions on the altar [vayaqter et-ha-chalavim ha-mizbeachah].

**21** And the breasts [ve-et-he-chazot] and the right thigh [ve-et-shok ha-yamin] Aharon waved [henif Aharon] as a wave offering [tenufah] before the LORD [lifney YHWH] — as Moshe commanded [ka'asher tsivvah Moshe].

---

**22** And Aharon lifted his hands [vayissa Aharon et-yadav] toward the people [el-ha-am] [vayissa Aharon et-yadav — "Aharon lifted his hands"; the gesture of the priestly blessing; the uplifted hands toward the people is the first liturgical act of the newly installed high priest toward the congregation; this gesture becomes the formal posture of the Aaronic benediction (Num 6:22-27) and is preserved in Jewish practice as the duchening gesture of the Kohanim to the present day] and blessed them [vayevarekhem] and he came down [vayered] from offering the sin offering [me'asot ha-chattat] and the burnt offering [ve-ha-olah] and the peace offerings [ve-ha-shelamim].

**23** And Moshe and Aharon went in [vayavo Moshe ve-Aharon] to the tent of meeting [el-ohel mo'ed] and they came out [vayyetse'u] and blessed the people [vayevarkhu et-ha-am] and the glory of the LORD [u-khevod YHWH] appeared to all the people [nir'ah el-kol-ha-am] [nir'ah el-kol-ha-am — "appeared to all the people"; nir'ah is the niphal of ra'ah, "to see" — "was seen" / "appeared"; the kevod YHWH visible to all Israel; the fulfillment of Moshe's promise in v.6; the appearance of the divine glory publicly validates the entire ordination process and Aharon's priestly installation].

**24** And fire came out [vattetse esh] [vattetse esh — "fire came out"; the same vocabulary as Lev 10:2 (the strange fire of Nadav and Abihu) and 2 Chr 7:1 (fire at Solomon's temple dedication); the divine fire here signals the LORD's own acceptance and consumption of the offerings — the altar fire, though previously lit (Lev 6:9, 12-13), is here powerfully confirmed as of heavenly origin; the parallel with Solomon's temple (2 Chr 7:1-3) indicates this moment at Sinai as the archetype of all subsequent divine-fire acceptances of legitimate worship in Yisra'el's history] from before the LORD [mi-lifney YHWH] and consumed [vattokal] upon the altar [al-ha-mizbeach] the burnt offering [et-ha-olah] and the fat portions [ve-et-ha-chalavim] and all the people saw [vayar kol-ha-am] and shouted [vayyaronnu] [vayyaronnu — from ranan, "to cry out with joy / to shout a ringing cry"; the verb denotes exuberant vocal response to divine action; this is not merely applause but the jubilant cry of a people witnessing the arrival of divine presence] and they fell [vayippelu] upon their faces [al-peneyhem] [vayippelu al-peneyhem — "they fell upon their faces"; the classic posture of prostration before the divine presence; the same response as Ezekiel at the Chebar vision (Ezek 1:28), as John at Patmos (Rev 1:17), and as the disciples at the transfiguration (Matt 17:6); the fall to the ground is the bodily confession that one stands in the presence of the living God].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Leningrad Codex B19A, Folios 62v–63r)

1. **v.1 — Ba-yom ha-shemini / "On the eighth day":** The chapter opens with the temporal marker וַיְהִי בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁמִינִי (vayehi ba-yom ha-shemini, "and it came to pass on the eighth day"). The construction vayehi + temporal phrase is a narrative opening formula (compare Gen 1:5; Ex 12:41; Josh 5:11) that marks the beginning of a new and significant episode. The numeral שְּׁמִינִי (shemini, "eighth") carries considerable theological weight in the Hebrew Bible. Seven is the number of divine completeness and rest (the Sabbath); eight therefore marks the day after completion — the day of new beginning. The eighth day recurs as a theologically charged moment throughout Leviticus and beyond: the eighth day of male circumcision (Lev 12:3), the eighth day after which a newborn animal may be sacrificed (Lev 22:27), the eighth day assembly (Shemini Atseret) after the seven days of Sukkot (Lev 23:36). In each case, the eighth day marks a transition into a new state: the ordained priest enters his office, the child enters the covenant community, the animal enters sacrificial eligibility, the festival cycle closes into new beginning. The LXX renders ba-yom ha-shemini as τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ὀγδόῃ (tē hēmera tē ogdoē, "on the eighth day"), faithfully preserving the ordinal.

2. **v.2 — Calf for Aharon's chattat:** The specification that Aharon's personal sin offering is an עֵגֶל בֶּן-בָּקָר (egel ben-baqar, "calf — son of the herd") rather than the full bull (par) of Lev 8:14 has puzzled commentators. The Leningrad Codex reads egel clearly; the LXX confirms μόσχον ἐκ βοῶν (moschon ek boōn, "a calf from the herd"). The rabbinic tradition (Talmud Yoma 2a; Abravanel) connects this calf specifically to the golden calf sin (Ex 32): Aharon's inaugural sin offering on the eighth day is a calf, recalling the calf he made, as a perpetual expiation carried into the priesthood. Whether or not this connection is original to the author, the detail is lexically preserved in both MT and LXX without harmonization to the Lev 8:14 par.

3. **vv.3-4 — Instructions to Yisra'el — four types of offering in one day:** The people's offerings on the eighth day are extraordinary in their variety and quantity: (1) a male goat for chattat; (2) a calf-and-lamb pair for olah; (3) an ox-and-ram pair for shelamim; (4) a minchah with oil. This fourfold offering on a single day is unprecedented in Leviticus — the combined sacrifice signals that the eighth day is not an ordinary day but the founding moment of the entire sacrificial order. The phrase כִּי הַיּוֹם יְהוָה נִרְאָה אֲלֵיכֶם (ki ha-yom YHWH nir'ah aleikhem, v.4, "for today the LORD is appearing to you") explicitly states the theological occasion: the offerings are not ends in themselves but the appointed means by which the LORD will make himself visible to the people. The LXX renders nir'ah aleikhem as ὀφθήσεται ὑμῖν κύριος (ophthēsetai hymin kyrios, "the LORD will appear to you"), using ὁράω (horaō) in the future passive — "will be seen by you" — which accurately captures the niphal future force of the Hebrew.

4. **v.7 — Aharon's first act at the altar:** The command to Aharon to "draw near to the altar" (qerav el-ha-mizbeach) is the formal commissioning of the high priest to his office. The verb קְרַב (qerav) — the qal imperative of qrv, "to draw near, approach" — is the same root as qorban ("offering"), which derives from "something brought near [to the LORD]." Aharon himself now "draws near" in the same sense as an offering draws near — he approaches the sacred space as one wholly given to the LORD's service. The double task of his inaugural offering — chattat and olah for himself first, then for the people — reflects the theological principle articulated in Heb 7:27: the high priest must first offer for his own sins before he can offer for the people. The phrase כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה (ka'asher tsivvah YHWH, "as the LORD commanded") at the end of v.7 grounds the entire ceremony in divine authorization, not priestly initiative.

5. **vv.8-11 — Aharon's chattat procedure:** The sin-offering blood rite for Aharon's personal chattat (vv.8-9) uses the dipping-and-smearing procedure on the altar horns rather than the sprinkling toward the veil (Lev 4:6) or the tent-of-meeting altar (Lev 4:18) — this places it at the level of the individual lay sin-offering of Lev 4:22-26 rather than the high-priestly Day of Atonement procedure of Lev 16. The flesh and skin are burned outside the camp (v.11), consistent with the sin-offering rule of Lev 4:11-12 and 8:17 for offerings whose blood is brought into the sanctuary precincts or used for atonement of the priesthood. The fat portions, kidneys, and liver lobe are burned on the altar (v.10) — again exactly per Lev 4:8-10 and the pattern established in Lev 8:16. The phrase ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et-Moshe ("as the LORD commanded Moshe") at v.10 is the compliance formula linking Aharon's inaugural performance to the Mosaic prescription — Aharon acts not by his own authority but in exact accordance with the instructions Moshe received from the LORD.

6. **v.15 — vaychatte'ehu / "he purified it":** The verb וַיְחַטְּאֵהוּ (vaychatte'ehu) in v.15 is the piel of חָטָא (chata') applied to the altar via the people's sin-offering goat. The suffix -ehu ("it") refers to the altar, not the goat: "he performed the chattat-rite upon it [the altar]." This piel verb (also used in Lev 8:15) is the technical term for the blood-manipulation rite by which sin-offering blood purges accumulated impurity from the altar. The phrase כָּרִאשׁוֹן (ka-rishon, "like the first") at the end of v.15 explicitly links the people's chattat procedure to the procedure Aharon performed for himself in vv.8-11 — the same blood manipulation applies. The LXX renders vaychatte'ehu as ἐκαθάρισεν αὐτό (ekatharisen auto, "he purified it"), using the verb καθαρίζω (katharizō, "to cleanse/purify"), consistent with its rendering of the same verb at Lev 8:15, and confirms that the action is purificatory in function.

7. **v.17 — Minchah from "the morning burnt offering" (olat ha-boqer):** The specification that the grain offering is presented mil-levad olat ha-boqer ("besides the burnt offering of the morning") is textually significant. The olat ha-boqer is the first of the two daily tamid offerings prescribed in Ex 29:38-42 — a yearling lamb with its grain offering and drink offering each morning. By noting that the eighth-day minchah is brought "besides" (mil-levad) the tamid, the text establishes that even on this extraordinary inaugural day, the regular daily worship is not suspended. This theological-liturgical principle — that special occasions supplement rather than replace the regular order of worship — is embedded in the sacrificial legislation from its very beginning. The LXX renders mil-levad as χωρὶς (chōris, "apart from / besides"), accurately capturing the additive rather than substitutive sense.

8. **v.22 — The first priestly blessing:** Aaron's lifting of his hands (וַיִּשָּׂא אַהֲרֹן אֶת-יָדָו, vayissa Aharon et-yadav) and his blessing of the people constitutes the first performance of the Aaronic blessing in biblical history. The exact content of this blessing is not given in Lev 9:22 — only the gesture and the act; the textual content of the Aaronic blessing is provided in Num 6:24-26 ("The LORD bless you and keep you..."). The Leningrad Codex reads et-yadav (singular in form, dual in sense — "his hands," both hands lifted), confirmed by the Vaticanus: ἐπάρας τὰς χεῖρας αὐτοῦ (eparas tas cheiras autou, "lifting his hands"). The gesture of upraised hands toward the people is the physical enactment of blessing flowing from the priest toward the congregation — as opposed to the raised hands in prayer or supplication, which are oriented upward toward God. After blessing, Aharon "came down" (vayered) from the altar — the verb implies a spatial descent from the altar platform, confirming that the ancient altar stood elevated above the courtyard ground level.

9. **v.23 — Moshe and Aharon enter together:** The entrance of both Moshe and Aharon into the tent of meeting (ohel mo'ed) in v.23 is unique in the Pentateuch — it is the only occasion where both Moshe (as the LORD's ordained mediator) and Aharon (as the newly installed high priest) enter the tent together. Commentators ancient and modern have speculated on what transpired inside: the Siphra (rabbinic commentary on Leviticus) suggests Moshe taught Aharon the incense offering procedure, which was the one priestly duty Aharon had not yet performed that day. The narrative leaves the content of the shared entrance unreported — what matters theologically is the transition of authority: when they emerge together and bless the people, the joint blessing signifies the completion of the handoff. Moshe's role as officiating priest ends; Aharon's begins. From this point forward in Leviticus and Numbers, it is Aharon (not Moshe) who ministers at the altar and in the tent. The LXX renders vayavo Moshe ve-Aharon as καὶ εἰσῆλθεν Μωυσῆς καὶ Ααρων (kai eisēlthen Mōusēs kai Aarōn, "and Moses and Aaron went in"), using a singular verb (εἰσῆλθεν) with two subjects — consistent with Hebrew grammar and not implying separate entries.

10. **v.24 — Fire from before the LORD / vattetse esh mi-lifney YHWH:** The verse's key phrase — וַתֵּצֵא אֵשׁ מִלִּפְנֵי יְהוָה (vattetse esh mi-lifney YHWH, "and fire came out from before the LORD") — is the theological and narrative apex of the entire book thus far. Three observations:

    (a) **"From before the LORD" (mi-lifney YHWH):** The preposition mi-lifney ("from before / from in front of") is directional — the fire comes out from the place of the LORD's presence (the tent of meeting, where the LORD's kavod has just appeared in v.23). The fire is not produced by human means (no kindling is mentioned) and is not a natural phenomenon but a divine emanation from the seat of the divine presence. The same phrase mi-lifney YHWH appears at Lev 10:2 (the deadly fire that destroys Nadav and Abihu), at Num 16:35 (the fire that destroys Korach's company), and at 2 Chr 7:1 (the fire at Solomon's temple dedication). The Lev 9:24 fire thus becomes the paradigmatic instance of divine fire: the fire that accepts legitimate worship, against which all subsequent divine-fire episodes are measured.

    (b) **"Consumed" (vattokal):** The verb וַתֹּאכַל (vattokal) is from אָכַל (akhal, "to eat/consume") — the fire "ate" the offerings. The same verb is used of the divine fire in Ex 24:17 ("the appearance of the glory of the LORD was like a devouring fire") and Deut 4:24 ("the LORD your God is a consuming fire"). The fire does not merely burn; it consumes — it is an act of divine reception. The LORD, through the fire, "eats" the portion given to him on the altar, as a host receives what is brought to his table.

    (c) **The people's response — vayyaronnu va-yippelu al-peneyhem:** The two verbs of v.24b — וַיָּרֹנּוּ (vayyaronnu, "they shouted/sang out") and וַיִּפְּלוּ עַל-פְּנֵיהֶם (vayippelu al-peneyhem, "they fell upon their faces") — are simultaneous and contradictory in physical terms (one cannot shout and lie prostrate at the same moment) but theologically complementary: the joyful acclamation of divine acceptance (the shout) paired with the awestruck prostration of creaturely unworthiness before the Holy. The same pairing of exultation and prostration appears at 2 Chr 7:3 at Solomon's temple dedication, which explicitly parallels Lev 9. The LXX renders vayyaronnu as ἠλάλαξαν (ēlalaxan, "they cried the alalā — a ritual shout of acclaim"), using a specifically cultic Greek term for the ritual shout (ἀλαλάζω, alalazō), which captures the liturgical character of the response. The LXX renders vayippelu as ἔπεσαν (epesan, "they fell"), using the simple aorist of πίπτω (piptō).

### LXX (Codex Vaticanus) Observations — Pages 93–94

The Vaticanus text for Lev 9 aligns closely with the MT throughout, with several notable features:

- **v.2 — "Calf" (egel) vs. "young bull" (par):** The LXX at v.2 reads μόσχον ἐκ βοῶν (moschon ek boōn, "a calf from the herd"), confirming the MT's egel (calf) against any tendency to harmonize to the par (bull/ox) of Lev 8:14. The Vaticanus text thus supports the lectio difficilior: the intentional use of "calf" here is preserved in both textual traditions.

- **v.3 — Se'ir izzim / "male goat":** The LXX renders se'ir izzim as χίμαρον ἐξ αἰγῶν (chimaron ex aigōn, "a he-goat from the goats"), using χίμαρος (chimaros, a young male goat). The MT se'ir means "hairy one / male goat" — the LXX's chimaros is a reasonable equivalence. Some later manuscripts (Alexandrinus) read τράγον (tragos, "he-goat"), the more common term; Vaticanus preserves the more specific chimaros.

- **v.7 — "Make atonement" (vekhapper):** The LXX renders vekhapper ba'adkha as ἐξίλασαι περὶ σεαυτοῦ (exilasai peri seautou, "make atonement concerning yourself"), using ἐξιλάσκομαι (exilaskomai, "to propitiate/expiate/make atonement"), the standard LXX equivalent of kipper throughout Leviticus. This Greek verb — the same root as ἱλαστήριον (hilastērion, "mercy seat" / "place of atonement") in Lev 16:2 and Rom 3:25 — is the term through which the New Testament theology of Christ's atonement inherits its vocabulary directly from the Levitical sacrificial system.

- **v.21 — Aharon waved / "the breasts and the right thigh":** The LXX at v.21 reads: καὶ τὸ στηθύνιον καὶ τὸν βραχίονα τὸν δεξιόν (kai to stēthynion kai ton brachiona ton dexion, "and the breast and the right arm/shoulder"). The MT has shok ha-yamin, typically translated "right thigh/leg" (from shaq, "leg/thigh"). The LXX's βραχίονα (brachiona, "arm/forearm") reflects a different reading tradition or anatomical interpretation of shok — some suggest the LXX here follows a tradition identifying the priestly portion as the upper foreleg rather than the haunch. The MT shok is consistently rendered elsewhere as βραχίων in the LXX (Lev 7:32-33; Num 18:18), so this is not an anomaly but a consistent LXX tradition for this term.

- **v.22 — "He lifted his hands" (et-yadav):** Vaticanus reads ἐπάρας τὰς χεῖρας αὐτοῦ ἐπ' αὐτούς (eparas tas cheiras autou ep' autous, "lifting his hands toward them"), adding the directional phrase "toward them" (ἐπ' αὐτούς) — which is implied in the MT but not explicit. The LXX addition clarifies the orientation of the gesture (toward the people) as distinct from hands raised in prayer (toward heaven). This directional nuance may reflect an early interpretive tradition in the LXX that preserved the physical understanding of the blessing gesture.

- **v.23 — "And the glory of the LORD appeared to all the people":** The LXX renders u-khevod YHWH nir'ah el-kol-ha-am as καὶ ὤφθη ἡ δόξα κυρίου παντὶ τῷ λαῷ (kai ōfthē hē doxa kyriou panti tō laō, "and the glory of the Lord was seen by all the people"). The aorist passive ὤφθη (ōfthē, "was seen / appeared") is the LXX's standard rendering of the niphal of ra'ah — it is also the verb used of the resurrection appearances of Jesus in 1 Cor 15:5-8 (ὤφθη Κηφᾷ, "he appeared to Cephas"). The LXX's consistent use of ὤφθη for YHWH's appearances in the Pentateuch thus provides the lexical background for the New Testament's language of resurrection appearance.

- **v.24 — "Fire came out from before the LORD":** Vaticanus reads καὶ ἐξῆλθεν πῦρ παρὰ κυρίου (kai exēlthen pyr para kyriou, "and fire came out from the LORD / from beside the LORD"). The LXX uses παρά (para, "from beside / from the presence of") rather than ἐκ προσώπου (ek prosōpou, "from the face of / from before"), which might be expected for mi-lifney. The para kyriou construction ("from the LORD") somewhat softens the spatial directionality of the Hebrew mi-lifney ("from before the face of"), but preserves the essential point that the fire's origin is the divine presence. At 2 Chr 7:1 (LXX), the same event at Solomon's temple uses κατέβη πῦρ ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ (katebē pyr ek tou ouranou, "fire descended from heaven") — a different spatial description that emphasizes vertical descent. The two LXX descriptions together construct the divine fire as both "from the LORD's presence" (Lev 9) and "from heaven" (2 Chr 7), consistent with the tabernacle-as-heaven symbolism throughout the priestly literature.

— EMET
